coredata.dc:title: In situ doping lignin-derived carbon quantum dots on magnetic hydrotalcite for enhanced degradation of Congo Red over a wide pH range and simultaneous removal of heavy metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Yaoqin
Xu, Hui
Wei, Shuxia
Jiang, Fangyuan
Zhang, Jiemei
Ge, Yuanyuan
Li, Zhili
coredata.dc:description: A new N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC composite was prepared by loading N, S carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) derived from lignin on magnetic hydrotalcite (HTC) via an in-situ growth method. The characterization results showed that the catalyst had a mesoporous structure. These pores facilitate the diffusion and mass transfer of pollutant molecules inside the catalyst, allowing them to approach the active site smoothly. The catalyst performed well in the UV degradation of Congo red (CR) over a wide pH range (3−11), with efficiencies over 95.43 % in all cases. Even at a high NaCl content (100 g/L), the catalyst showed extraordinary CR degradation (99.30 %). ESR analysis and free radical quenching experiments demonstrated that OH and O2 − were the main active species governing CR degradation. Besides, the composite had outstanding removal efficiency for Cu2+ (99.90 %) and Cd2+ (85.08 %) simultaneously due to the electrostatic attraction between the HTC and metal ions. Moreover, the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC had excellent stability and recyclability during five cycles, making it free of secondary contamination. This work provides a new environment-friendly catalyst for the simultaneous removal of multiple pollutants and a waste-to-waste strategy for the value-added utilization of lignin.

head.ce:title: In situ doping lignin-derived carbon quantum dots on magnetic hydrotalcite for enhanced degradation of Congo Red over a wide pH range and simultaneous removal of heavy metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology, Guangxi University, 100 Daxuedong Road, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Petrochemical Resource Processing and Process Intensification Technology, Guangxi University, 100 Daxuedong Road, Nanning 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC composite was prepared by loading N, S carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) derived from lignin on magnetic hydrotalcite (HTC) via an in-situ growth method. The characterization results showed that the catalyst had a mesoporous structure. These pores facilitate the diffusion and mass transfer of pollutant molecules inside the catalyst, allowing them to approach the active site smoothly. The catalyst performed well in the UV degradation of Congo red (CR) over a wide pH range (3−11), with efficiencies over 95.43 % in all cases. Even at a high NaCl content (100 g/L), the catalyst showed extraordinary CR degradation (99.30 %). ESR analysis and free radical quenching experiments demonstrated that OH and O2 − were the main active species governing CR degradation. Besides, the composite had outstanding removal efficiency for Cu2+ (99.90 %) and Cd2+ (85.08 %) simultaneously due to the electrostatic attraction between the HTC and metal ions. Moreover, the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC had excellent stability and recyclability during five cycles, making it free of secondary contamination. This work provides a new environment-friendly catalyst for the simultaneous removal of multiple pollutants and a waste-to-waste strategy for the value-added utilization of lignin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of the industry has caused severe water pollution. The pollutants contained in industrial wastewater not only damage the ecosystem but also endanger human health. The most notorious pollutants include heavy metal ions such as Cu2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, and Pb2+ [1]; and organic dye contaminants, such as Congo red (CR), methylene blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), and crystalline violet (CV) [2,3]. Due to the high stability of organic dyes [4], and the complex interaction between the organic and heavy metal ions, they are challenging to be entirely removed by conventional wastewater treatment processes, such as filtration, precipitation, adsorption, fungal immobilization, and membrane treatment [5–8]. Inspired, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) [9] have received wide attention in removing organic dyes due to the complete degradation of hazardous organics into CO2 and H2O. Among various AOPs processes, photocatalytic degradation is a green, low-cost, and highly efficient wastewater treatment technology that uses solar light to degrade organic contaminants in the presence of photocatalysts [10]. The extensively used photocatalysts encompass semiconductor materials, such as titanium dioxide (TiO2), zinc oxide (ZnO), copper oxide (CuO), and cadmium sulfide (CdS). However, the band gaps of these semiconductor materials are too broad and show relatively low photocatalytic activity, resulting in low degradation efficiency of organic dyes. Besides, these semiconductor materials contain heavy metal elements, which can easily leak and cause secondary pollution to the environment. Therefore, seeking new photocatalysts that are safe, non-toxic, and cost-effective is imperative.

Compared to other carbon-based photocatalysts (carbon nitride, graphene, and carbon nanotubes), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of 0-dimensional carbon material [11] with water solubility [12], safety [13], and adjustable composition [14]. In particular, the excellent optical and electronic properties of CQDs [15], including tunable photoluminescence (PL) [16] and extraordinary up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) for efficient light harvesting (from deep UV to near-infrared) make the sake of full utilization of the solar spectrum [17]. The CQDs have attracted increasing attention in photocatalysis [18], bioimaging [19], sensing [20], fluorescent probes [21], and other fields [22]. With the increasing resource shortage and environmental pollution, people gradually turn to biomass as carbon precursors for preparing CQDs, such as starch, glucose, bagasse, and lignin. Lignin is the second largest biomass resource in the plant world after cellulose [23], with a global production of about 50 million tons per year in pulping industry [24]. More than 98 % of lignin is discharged into rivers as black liquor or burned for heat [25,26], a severe waste of resources. High-value utilization of lignin is of great significance to the sustainable development of society [27,28]. Considering the unique aromatic rings structure in lignin [29], it has excellent potential to produce CQDs as a green and sustainable carbon source [30].

On the other hand, CQDs have high activity and small particle size, but they are accessible to aggregate, leading to inactivation and being difficult to recover [31]. Therefore, it needs to be dispersed and immobilized on suitable support [32]. Hydrotalcite (HTC) is a new type of inorganic material with a layered octahedral structure composed of a positively charged main layer and interlayer anions. It has the advantages of a large superficial surface area, low cost, and harmlessness. Accordingly, it has received wide attention in being used as catalyst support [33]. However, most active ingredients are loaded onto HTC by a post-deposition method, which results in low specific surface area and covering the active sites, thus decreasing the activity. Therefore, in this work, a new strategy based on in situ growing for immobilizing CQDs was proposed, which was anticipated to improve the dispersion and stability of CQDs and the activity of catalysts accordingly.

Base on the above consideration, alkali lignin was selected as the precursor carbon source to synthesize N and S co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs). The N, S-CQDs were loaded on the HTC by an in-situ growth method to improve the dispersion and stability of N, S-CQDs, thus improving the catalytic activity. The magnetic Fe3O4 was also introduced into the composite catalyst to prepare an N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC photocatalyst. The magnetic Fe3O4 not only facilitates photocatalyst recovery but also enhances the UV degradation effect. This study aims to understand: ①The effectiveness of the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC for UV degradation of CR. ②The active free radicals involved in the degradation process and the possible degradation mechanism. ③The potential of the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC for simultaneous removal of organic dyes and heavy metal ions and actual wastewater treatment.

Alkali lignin (AL) was purchased from TCI Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Thiourea, MgSO4⸱7H2O, Al2(SO4)3⸱18H2O, NaCO3, NaOH, NH3⸱H2O, K2Cr2O7 were supplied by Guanghua Chemical (Guangdong, China). CuSO4⸱5H2O, Cd(NO3)2⸱4H2O, FeCl3⸱6H2O, and FeCl2⸱4H2O were obtained from Macklin. Congo Red (CR) (C32H22N6Na2O6S2, Mw=696.68 g/mol, CAS No. 573-58-0) was supplied by Kermel (Tianjin, China), deionized water.

13.52 g of FeCl3⸱6H2O and 9.93 g of FeCl2⸱4H2O (nFe 3+: nFe 2+=1:1) were added in a 250 mL beaker and dissolved with 100 mL of deionized water. Then 30 mL of NH3⸱H2O was added to the baker. After the reaction, the precipitant was formed and collected for washing with deionized water and then dried overnight in an oven at 60 °C. The magnetic Fe3O4 powder was obtained.

1.00 g of AL, 0.20 g of thiourea, and 50 mL deionized water were added in a baker for stirring to make it mix evenly. The solution was transferred to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 12 h in a blast drying oven. The reactor was cooled to room temperature. After dialyzing with a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 72 h, the purified N, S-CQD solution was obtained. The CQDs were also prepared similarly without the addition of thiourea.

100 mL of purified N, S-CQD solution and 0.10 g of Fe3O4 powder were put into a three-necked flask with stirring. Then 10 mL of mixed alkaline solution (NaCO3, NaOH) was added to adjust the pH (pH≈10) of the solution. 6.60 g MgSO4⸱7H2O and 3.00 g Al2(SO4)3⸱18H2O were added into a 250 mL beaker and dissolved with 100 mL deionized water, which was instilled slowly into a three-necked, and the temperature was raised to 80 °C under stirring at 300 rpm. After reacting for 4 h, the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was formed and gathered for washing with deionized water and then dried overnight in an oven at 60 °C. For comparison, the CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was prepared analogously.

0.10 g catalyst and 50 mL of CR solution with an initial concentration of 50 mg/L of CR solution were put into a 100 mL beaker. The beaker was placed on a magnetic stirrer and stirred in the dark for 30 min (dark reaction) to achieve suction-desorption equilibrium. Then turned on the 20 W UV lamp (CNLIGHT ZW9D12A-U144) to provide light reacting for 120 min, extracted 3 mL of solution at certain time intervals, and used a UV-visible spectrophotometer (PERSEE TU-1810) to measure the concentration Ct of CR (λmax=497 nm). The removal rate (R) could be computed using the following formula (Eq. (1)).

The degradation kinetics were analyzed using the pseudo-first-order (Eq. (2)) and pseudo-second-order (Eq. (3)) models, respectively.

R (%) is the removal rate, C0, Ct (mg/L) are the original and measured consistencies of CR, k1 (min-1) and k2 (L·mg−1·min−1) are kinetic rate constants, respectively, and time is t (min).

The effects of the initial concentration of CR (30, 40, 50, 60, 70 mg/L), the addition of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC (1, 2, 4, 6 g/L), pH (3, 5, 7, 9, 11) and the content of NaCl (5, 10, 50, 100 g/L) on the UV degradation of CR (50 mL, 50 mg/L) by the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC were investigated. The UV degradation activity of the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was also investigated for the removal of MB, CV, mixed dyes (CR and MB mixed), and heavy metal ions (Cu2+, Cd2+, Cr2O7 2- 20 ppm) with CR.

The role of the free radicals generated during the reaction was explored using different quenching agents. From a review of the literature, it was known that tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) [34] was an effective quencher of OH, then Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) [35] and p-benzoquinone (BQ) [36] were effective in scavenging h+ and O2 -, respectively. Therefore, TBA, EDTA-2Na, and BQ were applied as free radical scavengers for the scavenging of
OH, h+, and
O2 -, respectively.
The used catalyst was collected and washed thrice with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, then dried overnight in a blast oven at 60 °C for next use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of CR in actual wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: Polluted water specimens were extracted from a pool on the West Campus of Guangxi University. Suspended solids in the polluted water specimens were filtrated by vacuum filtration. A 50 mg/L of CR solution (pH = 7) was prepared using pool water and preserved for use.

The surface morphology of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was observed with a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Gemini SEM 500). The N, S-CQDs were observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 2100F, Japan). The structures of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC, N, S-CQDs@HTC, and HTC were analyzed with an X-ray diffractometer (MiniFlex 600, Japan). The surface area and nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the catalyst were determined by a Micromeritics ASAP 2460 analyzer from the USA. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) was applied to analyze the composition and state of elements. The active free radical species generated in the reaction were qualitatively analyzed by electron spin resonance (ESR, Bruker A300, Germany) spectroscopy. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, STA449C, Netzsch) was carried out in an N2 atmosphere at a temperature range from 30 to 1000 °C. The heating rate was 10 °C/min, and the flow rate of N2 was 50 mL/min. The magnetic properties of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC were measured using a vibrating sample magnetometer (Quantum Design, USA). UV–vis absorbance was recorded using the MAPADA UV-6300 dual beam UV-visible spectrophotometer. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Agilent 720ES) was used to measure the concentration of heavy metal ions.

As seen in Fig. 1a, the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC had a layered structure with tight bonding and rough surface, which was conducive to loading N, S-CQDs, and the utilization of light sources. The TEM image (Fig. 1b) showed that the N, S-CQDs were spherical and uniformly dispersed with no agglomeration. The particle size distribution was in the range of 2-6 nm, as seen in the inset of Fig. 1b. Furthermore, the corresponding HRTEM image from Fig. 1c displayed the visible lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm, which was consistent with the lattice spacing of the (100) plane of ideal graphitic carbon [37]. Figs.1d-j presented the homologous EDS elemental monographs, which precisely reflected the partition of aluminum (Al), magnesium (Mg), carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), sulfur (S), and iron (Fe) in N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC.

The crystal structures of HTC, N, S-CQDs@HTC, and N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC were analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2a, the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 11.75°, 23.6°, 60.6°, and 62° could be attributed to the surfaces (003), (006), (110), and (113) of HTC (Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards No.38-0486) [38,39]. The diffraction peaks at N, S-CQDs@HTC and N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC at 26.5° and 45.6° pertained as distinct graphitic carbon (002) and (100) reflection peaks (PDF#41-1487) [40,41]. The reflection peaks at 30.0° and 35.4° could be incriminated to the (220) and (311) lattice planes of face-centered cubic spinel-type Fe3O4, indicating the presence of Fe3O4 (JCPDS No.88-0866) [42]. Furthermore, a narrow peak at 54.1° appeared in N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC, speculated to be the reflection peak of the (116) lattice face Fe2O3 (Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards No.04-0755). The crystallinity could be computed using the following formula (Eq. (4)) [43].
(4)
Area of crystalline peaks
Area of
all
peaks
crystalline + Amorphous
× 100 %

The crystallinity of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was 50.29 %, calculated by the formula (4). The catalyst with low crystallinity could offer numerous active sites for reactions [44] and make the effective charge separation and longer survival time of the photogenerated charge carriers [45].

The adsorption-desorption isotherms of N2, the specific surface area and the pore size arrangement were estimated based on the BET approach and the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method, respectively. Fig. 2b showed the isotherms represented a conventional Langmuir IV curve and a peculiar hysteresis curve (the H4 hysteresis loop), demonstrating that the composite had a mesoporous structure. The pore size distribution in the inset of Fig. 2b manifested a narrow pore size range (5-15 nm) of the composite, further proving the existence of mesoporous pores.

The functional groups in HTC, N, S-CQDs, and N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC were examined by FT-IR. Fig. 3a displayed the stretching vibration peaks of O-H (3480 cm-1) [46], C=O (1630 cm-1) [47], and C-O (1120 cm-1) [48] appearing in the spectra of the three materials. The spectra of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC showed the stretching vibration peaks of C=C (1500 cm-1) [48], C-N (1420 cm-1) [14], C-S/C=S (1275 cm-1) [49], which did not appear in HTC. A characteristic peak attributed to M-O (M=Fe, Al, Mg) also appeared in the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC from 500 to 750 cm-1, which was not found in the spectra of N, S-CQDs. The characteristic peaks of HTC, N, S-CQDs, and Fe3O4 appeared simultaneously in the FT-IR spectrum of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC, indicating that the three substances existed in the composite.

The XPS result of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was shown in Fig. 3b. The narrow peaks of C, N, O, Al, Mg, and Fe in the composite demonstrate that N, S-CQDs, and Fe3O4 were immobilized on HTC successfully. The high-resolution of C1s XPS constrictive spectrogram in Fig. 3c could be ascribed to O-C-O (287.9 eV), C-N/C-O (285.6 eV), and C-C/C=C (284.6 eV) [50]. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s in Fig. 3d was deconvoluted into N-H (401.6 eV) and C-N (398.9 eV) [48], explaining that the N element was successfully doped to CQDs. The high-resolution spectrogram of O1s in Fig. 3e was decomposed into three constituent peaks C-O (532.1 eV), C=O (531.6 eV), and Fe-O (530.2 eV) [51]. Fig. 3f displayed the peaks in the Fe2p1/2 XPS spectrum at 731.5, 725.5, and 723.3 eV were ascribed to Fe (II) (Fe 2p1/2 Sat), Fe (III) (Fe 2p1/2), Fe (II) (Fe 2p1/2), and Fe2p3/2 XPS spectrogram at 717.6, 711.5 and 709.7 eV corresponding to Fe (III) (Fe 2p3/2 Sat), Fe (III) (Fe 2p3/2), and Fe (II) (Fe 2p3/2) [42]. The results further proved that Fe3O4 was fixed in the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC, facilitating the catalyst recovery.

The mass loss of HTC and N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC were investigated using TG and DTG. Fig. 4a demonstrated that the two materials had similar mass loss peaks at around 100 °C due to the volatilization of water in the sample. The first mass loss at around 208 °C corresponded to the removal of water of crystallization and interlayer water. The second mass loss was also the significant mass loss at about 400 °C due to the reduction of hydroxyl groups and interlayer CO3 2- from the plates [52]. As to N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC, the mass loss at 650 °C and 850 °C might correspond to N, S-CQDs, and iron oxide decomposition [53].

The magnetic performance of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was measured through a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) at 298 K (-25000 Oe ∼ 25000 Oe). Fig. 4b showed that the saturation magnetization of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC counted up to 25 emu/g. The magnetic performances of the catalyst before and after the reaction were nearly constant. Its excellent magnetism could recycle fleetly and reduce secondary pollution to the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of different catalysts and lights for the removal of CR
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption performance of CR by HTC and N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was investigated. Fig. 5a showed that the adsorption efficiency of CR by HTC and N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was 65.30 %, explaining that loading other substances did not improve the adsorption capacity of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC. Notably, the UV degradation efficiency of CR by CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC and N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was 84.36 % and 95.43 %. The removal of CR by the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was increased because the introduction of N/S would add new active N/S-containing functional groups. The difference in electronegativity between N/S and C atoms might improve the separation of carriers to form more free radicals and enhance the UV degradation effect [54]. Therefore, the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was selected as a photocatalyst for the experiments. To study the optical property of the photocatalyst, UV/Vis DRS (Shimadzu UV-3600 Japan) was collected for the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC. As illustrated in Fig. S1a, the catalyst showed strong visible-light absorption with an absorption edge at approximately 700 nm, demonstrating excellent visible-light absorption properties. Based on the UV/vis DRS, the band gap of the catalyst could be determined by the Kubelka-Munk function. As shown in Fig. S1b, according to the x-axis intercept of the linear portion of the plot, the band gaps of the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC were estimated to be 1.49 eV. Compared to the bandgap of CQDs (3.94 eV) [55], N-CQDs (2.98 eV) [56], S-CQDs (2.38 eV) [48], N, S-CQDs (1.75 eV) [57] and CQDs@γ-Fe2O3 (2.40 eV) [58], the catalyst had a smaller band gap, which proved that the catalyst has higher photocatalytic degradation activity. The degradation activity of the catalyst in different lights was compared. As seen in Fig. 5b, the UV degradation efficiency of the catalyst in UV light, visible light (200W 420nm), and sunlight was 95.43 %, 86.97 %, and 92.18 %, respectively. Although the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC could be excited under visible light (Eg<3.0 eV), it had excellent degradation efficiency in UV light because UV light irradiation could regenerate oxygen vacancies in the catalyst, which could promote the degradation of CR [59,60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV degradation of CR by the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6a displayed that with the increased initial CR concentration from 30 mg/L to 70 mg/L, the UV degradation efficiency of CR by N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC decreased from 98.91 % to 49.27 %. The kinetic simulation parameters were presented in Fig. S2 and Table S1. By contrast with the correlation coefficients R2, all the R2 of the pseudo-first-order could reach 0.99, which was more extensive than the R2 of the pseudo-second-order, explaining the UV degradation process of CR was more suitable to the pseudo-first-order model. As shown in Table S1, k1 decreased from 0.0346 min-1 to 0.0038 min-1 with the increased initial CR concentration from 30 mg/L to 70 mg/L. The number of active sites and the emergence of free radicals were also limited when the amount of catalyst was constant. The increased amount of CR caused its adsorption of photons. It severely suppressed the generation of photogenerated electrons (e-)-holes (h+), leading to less free radical production and a significant decrease in degradation efficiency [61]. Nevertheless, the degradation efficiency reduced slightly from 98.91 % to 95.43 % with the initial CR concentration from 30 mg/L to 50 mg/L. Considering practical benefits, 50 mg/L was chosen as the initial concentration for the subsequent reaction.

The dosage of the catalyst is one of the essential factors affecting degradation efficiency. Fig. 6b displayed the effect of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC dosage on UV degradation efficiency for CR. When the catalyst dosage increased from 1 g/L to 2 g/L, the UV degradation efficiency of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC for CR increased significantly from 48.53 % to 95.43 %. The degradation efficiency decreased from 95.43 % to 70.03 %, with the dosage increased from 2 g/L to 6 g/L. The pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic simulation parameters were presented in Fig. S3 and Table S2, respectively. The correlation coefficients R2 of the pseudo-first-order model (0.989-0.991) were more significant than the R2 of the pseudo-second-order model (0.884-0.890), demonstrating that the UV degradation reactions of CR were more suitable to the pseudo-first-order model. When the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC dosage increased from 1 g/L to 2 g/L, k1 increased from 0.0046 min−1 to 0.0243 min−1, indicating the more active sites were provided and free radicals were catalytic by the increase catalyst dosage, which contributed to the accelerated degradation of CR. With the catalyst dosage increased from 2 g/L to 6 g/L, k1 decreased from 0.0243 min-1 to 0.088 min-1, demonstrating that more catalysts would produce an agglomeration effect in reducing the transmittance of the solution, which caused light scattering. The light did not reach the interior of the solution or get a small amount, resulting in a weakened catalytic effect [62–64]. Considering the economic benefits, 2 g/L of catalyst dosage was selected for subsequent experiments.

The pH value is one of the essential factors affecting the composite surface charge and the nature of pollutants. The effect of pH values on the UV degradation of CR in the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC was investigated, as shown in Fig. 6c. When pH values increased from 3 to 7, the UV degradation efficiency of CR decreased from 99.34 % to 95.43 %. The removal of CR rose from 95.43 % to 99.34 %, with the pH values increased from 7 to 11. In addition, CR is positively charged under acidic conditions, while HTC has alkaline sites (-OH) that can attract protons [65,66]. Accordingly, CR molecules were more easily adsorbed onto N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC due to electrostatic effects, which accelerated the degradation of CR and improved the degradation efficiency [67]. Under alkaline conditions, the protonation-deprotonation process in the oxygen group regulated the surface charge to promote UV degradation [68]. The photogenerated electrons and holes were effectively separated by changing the charge state of the oxygen functional group in the catalyst. Thus, the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC could catalyze the production of more free radicals to promote the degradation of CR [69]. The UV degradation efficiency of CR by the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC reached over 95.43 % in a wide pH range, indicating that the catalyst had broad applicability.

Industrial wastewater contains organic dyes and a large amount of NaCl, which will affect the UV degradation of CR by the catalyst. Different amounts of NaCl were appended to the CR solution to investigate the salt tolerance of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC, as displayed in Fig. 6d. With the NaCl content increased from 0 to 10 g/L, the UV degradation efficiency of CR decreased from 95.43 % to 92.83 %, but the degradation efficiency was 99.30 % by adding more NaCl (50 g/L-100 g/L). The pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic simulation parameters were presented in Fig. S4 and Table S3, respectively. The UV degradation process of CR fitted better with the pseudo-first-order model, as the R2 exceeded 0.99 in all cases. When the NaCl content increased from 0 to 10 g/L, k1 decreased from 0.0243 min-1 to 0.0201 min-1. It is noteworthy that k1 increased from 0.0201 min-1 to 0.0387 min-1 as the NaCl content increased from 10 g/L to 100 g/L. The chloride ion in the solution had a quenching effect on the free radical (
OH) when at low NaCl content (< 10 g/L), which caused the decreasing UV degradation efficiency of CR [70]. On the contrary, more chloride ions in the solution could promote the generation of active chlorine species with increased NaCl content (> 50 g/L). The active chlorine species had excellent oxidizing properties, which played a synergistic role with oxygen-containing radicals to promote the UV degradation removal of CR by the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC. During the reaction, the production of active chlorine species was speculated by Eqs. (5)-(14) [71,72].

An electron spin resonance (ESR) experiment was carried out to detect the existence of reactive chlorine species using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin-trapping agent. Fig. 7a demonstrated that the (1:2:2:1) quadruple peak signal of DMPO-
OH [73] gradually disappears with the addition of NaCl, confirming that the chloride ion had a quenching effect on the hydroxyl radicals. Fig. 7b showed that the DMPO-ClO
double peak signal (1:1) and the DMPO-OClO
triple peak signal (1,1:1) were observed when the NaCl content was 100 g/L [74]. On the contrary, no signal was observed when the NaCl content was 10 g/L. It was further confirmed that more chloride ions could promote the production of ClO
and ClO2
- active species. The results indicated that promoting the UV degradation of CR by the catalyst could be achieved with high NaCl content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of other dyes and simultaneously removal of CR and heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: MB, MO, and CV were selected as cationic dyes to investigate the UV degradation performance of N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC composite. Fig. 8a demonstrated that the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC represented comparably excellent degradation efficiency (R > 95.43 %) for both anionic and cationic dyes. At the same time, CR and MB were mixed with investigating the selective degradation of the catalyst. The UV spectrum of the degraded solution was scanned at 200-800 nm by a UV-visible spectrophotometer. As seen in Fig. 8b, the mixed solution had characteristic adsorption peaks at 300 nm and 497 nm, respectively. Its characteristic absorption peaks disappeared after the UV degradation of the mixed solution by the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC, indicating that both dyes were degraded. The N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC had excellent UV degradation efficiency of two types of dyes. Moreover, no other distinct characteristic absorption peaks appeared at 200-800 nm, explaining that the UV degradation of organic dyes did not produce other intermediates and would not cause secondary environmental pollution.

Sewage contains dyes and various heavy metal ions. The heavy metal ions Cu2+, Cd2+, and Cr2O7 2- (C0=20 ppm) were added to the CR solution to investigate the ability of the catalyst to degrade CR and remove heavy metal ions simultaneously. Fig. 8c showed that the removal of Cu2+ was as high as 99.90 %, while the removal of Cd2+ and Cr2O7 2- was 85.08 % and 41.96 %, respectively. The HTC in the catalyst had acid-base duality, which could adsorb heavy metal ions through surface complexation and electrostatic effects, indicating that the catalyst demonstrated the capability to remove heavy metal ions from water [75]. The higher removal efficiency of CR (100 %) after mixing with heavy metal ions was because Cu2+ captured photogenerated electrons to form Cu+ with catalytic properties [76]. The Cu+ acted synergistically with the free radicals to degrade CR and enhanced the effect of degradation. In the presence of Cd2+ and Cr2O7 2-, the catalyst still showed significant degradation of CR (95.43 %), indicating that heavy metal ions did not inhibit the removal of CR. The results demonstrated that the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC had superior efficiency in removing heavy metal ions and CR simultaneously, indicating that the catalyst had a broad potential for application in treating multiple pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of CR in actual wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: As seen in Fig. 8d, compared with the simulated wastewater (95.43 %), the UV degradation efficiency of CR by the catalyst in the actual wastewater (90.88 %) was slightly reduced. However, 90.88 % of CR could be degraded in actual wastewater, indicating that the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC could be applied to treat actual wastewater.

To identify the ROS that participated in the UV degradation process of CR by the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC, ESR experiments were conducted using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO) as spin-trapping agents, respectively. As shown in Figs. 9a-c, a quadruple spike semaphore of DMPO-
OH (1:2:2:1), a quadruple peak semaphore of DMPO-
O2 - (1:1:1:1), and a triple peak signal TEMPO-h+ (1:1:1) could be observed, respectively [73] [77]. The results revealed that
OH,
O2 -, and h+ existed in the UV degradation of CR. Free radical quenching experiments were carried out to evaluate the impacts of
OH,
O2 -, and h+ in the UV degradation of CR by the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC. As shown in Fig. 10 , the degradation efficiency decreased from 95.43 % to 77.52 % after adding 1 mM of TBA. In comparison, the other two experimental groups showed significant changes in the degradation efficiency from 95.43 % to 42.35 % and 39.74 % after adding 1 mM of BQ and EDTA-2Na, respectively. It was demonstrated that both
OH,
O2 -, and h+ played essential roles in the UV degradation process of CR.

The vital index to measure the actual performance of the composite is its reusability and stability. Due to its great magnetism and stability, the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC could be easily recovered and regenerated by washing with deionized water. Fig. 11 demonstrated that after five cycles, the UV degradation efficiency of CR by the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC on CR gradually decreased from 95.43 % to 90.87 %. By comparing the FT-IR spectra (Fig.S5) of the catalyst before and after the reaction, it could be seen that the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC before and after the UV degradation had the same functional group, which did not change after the reaction, indicating that the catalyst had a stable structure. Fig.S6 displayed that the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC before and after the reaction had the same elemental composition, explaining that the surface elemental composition of the catalyst had not changed. Table S4 showed the changes in element content before and after UV degradation. The content of C/O/Fe slightly decreased by 2.46 %, 0.77 %, and 0.1 %, respectively. ICP-MS showed that the Fe3+ leaching amount was negligible (< 0.02 mg/L). The FT-IR and XPS explained the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC had good mechanical robustness and ultralong stability. The decrease in degradation efficiency might be due to some N, S-CQDs being shed during the cleaning process, which decreased the number of free radicals. After five repeated experiments, 90.87 % of CR could still be degraded by the catalyst, indicating that the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC had excellent reusability. ICP-MS was also used to measure the Fe3+ leaching amount, showing that the amount was negligible (< 0.02 mg/L). The results of ICP-MS were consistent with VSM, proving that the used catalyst still had excellent magnetic properties and was easy to recover.

The UV degradation mechanism consists of four key steps, namely, photo harvesting (step 1), electron excitation (step 2), electron and hole migration (steps 3 and 4), and surface catalytic chemical reactions (steps 5 and 6) [78], as depicted in Fig. 12 . The UV degradation of CR was induced by the photogenerated electrons (e-)-holes (h+) generated. When UV light irradiated the catalyst, the photogenerated electrons (e-)-holes (h+) pairs were generated in the conduction and valence bands. Subsequently, the e- and h+ migrated to the N, S-CQDs surfaces for oxidation and reduction reactions to generate free radical molecules. The h+ could further oxidize H2O to form
OH due to its strong oxidation ability, while e- reacted with O2 to form
O2 -. Both
O2 - and
OH had superior oxidizing properties that would attack the CR and degrade it into CO2 and H2O. In addition, the h+ could also directly oxidize the CR because the h+ with powerful oxidizing capacity. The results were consistent with the quenching experiment, indicating that both
OH,
O2 -, and h+ played essential roles in the UV degradation process of CR. The excitation of the catalyst by UV irradiation could be represented as follows (Eqs. (15–18)).
(15)
N , S − CQDs + hv →
e −
+
h +
(16)

(17)

(18)

+ ˙ OH + CR →
H 2

CO 2

An effective way to improve the UV degradation efficiency of CR is to reduce electron-hole pairs recombination. Many defect sites in N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC affected their photocatalytic properties. Surface defects could quickly capture and then release photogenerated charges, accelerating the separation of electrons and holes. In addition, surface defects are the adsorption site of O2, which reacts with surface photo-generated electrons to form O2 −, and O2 − will degrade organic pollutants [79]. The Oxygen vacancies can capture electrons to create peroxide and promote the oxidative degradation of CR [80]. Meanwhile, the point defects (N doping) improve the migration of photogenerated electron pairs at the interface and effectively inhibit its recombination [81]. The UV-vis absorption spectra of CQDs and N, S-CQDs were shown in Fig. 13a. The K-ribbon resorption (π→π*, conjugated construction) and R-ribbon resorption (n→π*, C=N) of N, S-CQDs were great reinforcement in the wavelength range of 225-290 nm, forming more free-flowing π-electrons and enhancing the UV degradation effect [82]. N and S elements strongly interacted with CQDs, which could promote electron transfer and reduce electron-hole pairs recombination significantly [83]. The transient photocurrent detection was performed to further investigate the electron-hole pairs separation and transfer efficiency of the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC, and N, S-CQDs@HTC. Photocurrent testing proceeded on an electrochemical operator station (CHI-660E, Shanghai Chenhua, China) with a 20W UV lamp at 365nm. The electrodes were prepared with the following methods. 0.1 g photocatalyst powder was dispersed into 200 μL of dimethylformamide (DMF) and 20 μL Nafion reagent under ultrasonication. Then the mixture was coated on a 2 cm × 2 cm FTO vitreous electrode which serves as the working electrode. An SCE electrode serves as the reference electrode, and a 2 cm × 2 cm Pt sheet was the counter electrode. As seen in Fig. 13b, the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC exhibited the highest ultraviolet excitation transient photocurrent, implicating the most significant density of charges. In addition, compared to the N, S-CQDs@HTC, the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC displayed the most stable photocurrent intensity, indicating that the electron-hole pairs in the ternary catalyst had a higher and more stable separation rate. The presence of Fe3O4 promoted the transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and reduced the recombination of electron-hole pairs significantly. The results showed that the N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC had excellent degradation efficiency due to the outstanding and stabilized separation efficiency for photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

In summary, N, S-CQDs were prepared using alkali lignin as a carbon source and thiourea as a passivator and then loaded onto magnetic HTC by an in-situ growth method. The catalyst had a mesoporous structure conducive to the CR approaching the active sites. The catalyst showed high degradation efficiency (> 95.43 %) of CR (50 mg/L) over a wide pH range in all cases. Moreover, the catalyst had an excellent salt tolerance, which displayed an incredible degradation efficiency (99.30 %) of CR at the high NaCl content (100 g/L). The N, S-CQDs@Fe3O4@HTC could simultaneously remove different types of dyes and heavy metal ions in the solution, demonstrating an excellent capacity of the catalyst with different pollutants treatment. The catalyst showed excellent reusability in the UV degradation process, and the degradation efficiency of CR could still reach 90 % after five cycles. The co-doping of N/S and Fe3O4 improved the separation and migration efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, providing the possibility of efficient degradation of CR. This work provides an efficient way to utilize lignin. It offers a green and efficient UV degradation catalyst that can be applied in continuous wastewater treatment.

Jiemei Zhang: Sampling, Data analysis. Yuanyuan Ge: Supervision, Data analysis. Zhili Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration.
